options: Shake the inhaler vigorously before use
Inhale rapidly and deeply while pressing the canister
Hold breath for 10 seconds after inhaling
Wait 1 minute between puffs if multiple puffs are ordered
Rinse the mouth with water after using a corticosteroid inhaler
questions.explanation: Correct MDI use includes shaking the inhaler, inhaling *slowly* and deeply, holding the breath for 10 seconds, waiting 1 minute between puffs, and rinsing the mouth after corticosteroid use to prevent thrush.

title: CV Pathologies & Procedures - Part B
description: ACS, Heart Failure, Hypertensive Crisis, PAD, DVT

questions.text: What is the key difference between STEMI and NSTEMI?
options: STEMI involves full-thickness vessel blockage requiring immediate reperfusion (Cath Lab)
STEMI is treated with pills only
questions.explanation: STEMI = ST Elevation = Complete Blockage. NSTEMI = Partial Blockage.

questions.text: Cardiogenic Shock is characterized by:
options: Pump failure causing severe Hypotension, tachycardia, and cold/clammy extremities
questions.explanation: The heart fails as a pump. Perfusion crashes globally.

questions.text: What is the primary function of an Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP)?
options: Inflates during diastole to perfuse coronaries, deflates during systole to reduce workload
questions.explanation: It helps the heart rest and feeds the heart muscle itself.

questions.text: Left-Sided Heart Failure typically causes symptoms in the:
questions.explanation: Left = Lungs. Blood backs up from the left ventricle into the pulmonary veins.

questions.text: Right-Sided Heart Failure typically causes:
questions.explanation: Right = Rest of Body. Blood backs up into the venous system.

questions.text: Classic sign of Acute Pulmonary Edema is:
questions.explanation: Fluid fills the alveoli, mixing with air to create pink foam.

questions.text: The priority nursing intervention for Pulmonary Edema is:
options: High Fowler's position (Sit up), Oxygen, Diuretics (Lasix)
questions.explanation: Sit up to drop the diaphragm. Diuretics to remove fluid. Morphine to reduce anxiety/preload.

questions.text: Hypertensive Emergency differs from Urgency because Emergency includes:
options: Evidence of Acute End-Organ Damage (Stroke, Kidney Failure, MI)
questions.explanation: Emergency = Organ Damage occurring NOW.

questions.text: In Hypertensive Urgency, the goal is to lower BP:
options: Slowly over 24 hours (approx 1/3 reduction) to prevent cerebral ischemia
questions.explanation: Dropping BP too fast can cause a stroke because the brain has auto-regulated to the high pressure.

questions.text: An Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) of < 0.5 indicates:
questions.explanation: Below 0.9 is PAD. Below 0.5 is severe ischemia.

questions.text: Intermittent Claudication is:
options: Pain/Cramping in legs during walking that relieves with rest (classic PAD sign)
questions.explanation: Leg angina. Muscles starve for O2 when walking.

questions.text: Which position helps relieve pain in Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD)?
questions.explanation: Gravity helps blood flow DOWN to the ischemic feet.

questions.text: After an Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) repair, why is hip flexion avoided?
options: It puts pressure on the femoral artery graft/incision
questions.explanation: Keep HOB < 45 degrees. Pinching the graft could rupture it.

questions.text: Virchow's Triad for DVT risk includes:
questions.explanation: Stasis (slow blood), Injury (vessel damage), Thick blood (clotting).

questions.text: Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs) are used to:
options: Prevent DVT by simulating walking/pumping blood
questions.explanation: NEVER put SCDs on a leg with a KNOWN DVT (it could dislodge the clot). Use for prevention only.

questions.text: A 'Papillary Muscle Rupture' is a catastrophic complication that can occur:
options: Days after an MI, causing acute valve failure/heart failure
questions.explanation: The muscle holding the mitral valve snaps. Massive backflow -> Flash Pulmonary Edema.

questions.text: MONA is an acronym for MI treatment. It stands for:
questions.explanation: Standard initial interventions for chest pain (though order varies).

questions.text: Nitroglycerin works for angina by:
options: Vasodilation (opening vessels) to increase O2 supply and decrease workload
questions.explanation: It opens the pipes, reducing the pressure the heart has to pump against (afterload) and filling pressure (preload).

questions.text: A common side effect of Nitroglycerin is:
questions.explanation: Vasodilation in the head causes a pounding headache.

questions.text: Why must a patient with a DVT be on bedrest initially (or limited activity)?
options: To prevent the clot from dislodging and becoming a Pulmonary Embolism (PE)
questions.explanation: Movement creates muscle pump action which can shoot the clot to the lungs.

questions.text: Signs of a Pulmonary Embolism (PE) include:
options: Sudden onset Dyspnea, Chest Pain, Tachycardia, Anxiety ('Doom')
questions.explanation: Clot in lung = No oxygen exchange + Right heart strain.

questions.text: Digital Clubbing is a sign of:
questions.explanation: Seen in chronic heart/lung disease (e.g., Tetraology of Fallot, COPD).

questions.text: Raynaud's Phenomenon involves:
options: Vasospasm in fingers/toes causing color changes (White-Blue-Red) in cold/stress
questions.explanation: Arteries spasm shut. Keep hands warm.

questions.text: Buerger's Disease is strongly linked to:
questions.explanation: Thromboangiitis obliterans. The only cure is to STOP SMOKING.

questions.text: Which position is best for a patient in Hypovolemic Shock?
questions.explanation: Gravity helps drain blood from legs back to the heart/brain.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse is analyzing an ECG strip and notes an irregular rhythm with absent P waves and a normal QRS complex. This rhythm indicates:
questions.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is characterized by an irregular rhythm and absent P waves (replaced by fibrillatory waves).

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which findings in an arterial blood gas (ABG) indicate respiratory acidosis? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Respiratory acidosis is defined by a low pH (<7.35) and a high PaCO2 (>45 mm Hg), with normal or elevated HCO3 if compensating.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following medications are considered high-alert medications and require at least independent double-checks before administration? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: High-alert medications carry a high risk of causing significant patient harm if used in error. Examples include IV insulin, IV anticoagulants (heparin), and IV concentrated electrolytes (potassium chloride).

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A client is prescribed a broad-spectrum antibiotic. The nurse should monitor for which of the following superinfections? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Broad-spectrum antibiotics can alter normal flora, leading to superinfections like oral candidiasis, C. diff, and vaginal yeast infections. MRSA and S. pneumoniae are not typically classified as superinfections in this context.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] The nurse is caring for a client with a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) receiving a heparin infusion. Which actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
options: Massage the affected leg to promote circulation
Apply a warm, moist compress to the affected area
questions.explanation: For a DVT, the nurse should monitor aPTT, elevate the leg, assess for bleeding, and apply a warm compress. Massaging the leg is contraindicated as it can dislodge the clot.

title: Respiratory & Neurological - Part A
description: COPD, Asthma, Pneumonia, Delirium, Dementia, Stroke, Seizures

questions.text: A patient with COPD has chronic bronchitis. What is the primary pathophysiological mechanism?
options: Chronic inflammation with excessive mucus production
questions.explanation: Chronic bronchitis is fundamentally an inflammatory issue with excessive mucus production and impaired ciliary function, causing airway obstruction.

questions.text: What distinguishes emphysema from chronic bronchitis in COPD?
options: Emphysema involves irreversible destruction of alveoli and loss of elastic recoil
questions.explanation: Emphysema is pure structural failure with irreversible destruction of alveoli, causing loss of elastic recoil and difficulty exhaling.

questions.text: A COPD patient has difficulty exhaling fully. What complication is most likely?
options: Air trapping and hypercapnia leading to respiratory acidosis
questions.explanation: Emphysema causes difficulty exhaling, leading to air trapping, CO2 retention (hypercapnia), and eventually respiratory acidosis.

questions.text: What is the FEV1/FVC ratio used to assess in COPD?
questions.explanation: The FEV1/FVC ratio measures how much air can be forced out in one second compared to total exhaled volume, indicating severity of obstruction.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY disease modifier for COPD?
questions.explanation: Smoking cessation is the number one intervention that can actually modify disease progression in COPD.

questions.text: Status asthmaticus is defined as:
options: Mild asthma requiring occasional inhaler use
Severe acute asthma attack not responding to conventional therapy
questions.explanation: Status asthmaticus is a life-threatening severe acute attack that does not respond to rescue inhalers or conventional treatment.

questions.text: What is pulsus paradoxus and why is it significant in status asthmaticus?
options: Drop in systolic BP >10 mmHg during inspiration, indicating severe obstruction
questions.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus is a drop in systolic BP >10 mmHg during inspiration, caused by increased intrathoracic pressure compressing the heart.

questions.text: What clinical sign indicates imminent respiratory failure in status asthmaticus?
options: Muscle fatigue, declining mental status, or cardiac arrest
questions.explanation: Muscle fatigue from the work of breathing, altered mental status, or cardiac arrest signal imminent respiratory failure requiring ventilation.

questions.text: What makes aspiration pneumonia unique compared to other pneumonias?
options: It starts as a chemical burn if pH <2.5, causing immediate lung tissue injury
It only affects the upper airways
It's easily treated with antibiotics alone
questions.explanation: Aspiration of acidic stomach contents (pH <2.5) causes immediate chemical injury to lung tissue before bacterial infection develops.

questions.text: What is the primary nursing intervention to prevent respiratory failure?
options: Turn, position, ambulate patients; encourage deep breathing and coughing
questions.explanation: Turning, positioning, ambulation, and encouraging deep breathing/coughing prevent fluid pooling and maintain airway clearance.

questions.text: What is the key difference between delirium and dementia?
options: Delirium is acute, sudden, fluctuating, and usually reversible
questions.explanation: Delirium is acute, sudden, and fluctuating with reversible causes (UTI, drugs, electrolytes). Dementia is chronic and progressive.

questions.text: What is the appropriate intervention for delirium?
options: Identify and correct the underlying cause (UTI, electrolytes, drugs)
questions.explanation: The priority is identifying and treating the underlying cause. Sedatives can worsen confusion, especially in elderly patients.

questions.text: Why should sedating medications be avoided in elderly patients with delirium?
options: They mask the metabolic chaos without fixing it and can worsen confusion
questions.explanation: Sedatives like Trazodone or Lorazepam mask symptoms without treating the cause and often worsen confusion in older adults.

questions.text: What percentage of strokes are ischemic versus hemorrhagic?
questions.explanation: The 80/20 rule: 80% of strokes are ischemic (blockage), while 20% are hemorrhagic (ruptured artery).

questions.text: Why is hemorrhagic stroke immediately dangerous?
options: Bleeding adds volume to the closed skull, rapidly increasing intracranial pressure
questions.explanation: Hemorrhagic stroke adds blood volume to the closed skull, causing rapid ICP increase and brain compression.

questions.text: Right hemisphere stroke causes deficits on which side of the body?
questions.explanation: Right hemisphere damage causes left-sided paralysis and sensory deficits, plus issues with spatial judgment and visual neglect.

questions.text: Left hemisphere stroke critically affects which functions?
questions.explanation: Left hemisphere damage affects the right side of the body and critically compromises speech, language, and mathematical abilities.

questions.text: What is a TIA and why is it significant?
options: A minor stroke with no consequences
Transient ischemic attack - symptoms resolve but signal extremely high risk for major stroke
questions.explanation: TIA symptoms resolve completely but serve as a critical warning sign of imminent major stroke risk.

questions.text: What is the time window for administering tPA in ischemic stroke?
questions.explanation: tPA (tissue plasminogen activator) must be given within 3-4.5 hours of symptom onset to be effective. Time is brain!

questions.text: Why is tPA contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke?
options: It's a clot-buster that would worsen active bleeding in the brain
questions.explanation: tPA breaks down clots. Giving it to someone with active brain bleeding would be fatal by preventing clot formation.

questions.text: What is the primary intervention for hemorrhagic stroke?
options: Strict blood pressure control to stop bleeding
questions.explanation: Hemorrhagic stroke requires strict BP control to stop bleeding, plus ICP management with positioning and hypertonic fluids.

questions.text: Why are hypertonic fluids (mannitol, hypertonic saline) used for increased ICP?
options: To create an osmotic gradient that pulls fluid out of swollen brain tissue
questions.explanation: Hypertonic fluids create an osmotic gradient to pull fluid from the brain compartment, reducing swelling without dropping systemic BP.

questions.text: What distinguishes partial seizures from generalized seizures?
options: Partial seizures start in one localized spot; generalized involve both hemispheres
Partial seizures only occur in children
questions.explanation: Partial seizures originate in one localized area, while generalized seizures (like tonic-clonic) involve both brain hemispheres.

questions.text: Status epilepticus is defined as:
options: Seizure lasting >5 minutes OR recurrent seizures without regaining consciousness
questions.explanation: Status epilepticus is a seizure >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without recovery of consciousness - a medical emergency.

questions.text: Why is status epilepticus life-threatening?
options: Sustained muscle activity burns through oxygen, causing hypoxia and potential respiratory failure
questions.explanation: Uncontrolled muscle activity depletes oxygen, leading to hypoxia, exhaustion, and potentially fatal respiratory failure.

questions.text: What is the first-line medication for status epilepticus?
questions.explanation: Rapid-acting benzodiazepines like lorazepam are given every 5 minutes to stop the electrical storm and prevent respiratory failure.

questions.text: A patient with COPD is prescribed albuterol. What is its mechanism of action?
questions.explanation: Albuterol is a bronchodilator that relaxes airway smooth muscle to improve airflow in COPD and asthma.

questions.text: What surgical option removes large non-functional air sacs in severe emphysema?
questions.explanation: Bullectomy removes giant non-functional air sacs (bullae) in severe emphysema to improve lung function.

questions.text: What type of dementia is characterized by fluctuating symptoms and visual hallucinations?
questions.explanation: Dementia with Lewy bodies presents with fluctuating cognition and complex visual hallucinations.

questions.text: Vascular dementia is caused by:
questions.explanation: Vascular dementia results from multiple small strokes that cumulatively damage brain tissue over time.

questions.text: What is the most common type of dementia?
questions.explanation: Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause of dementia, characterized by progressive cognitive decline.

questions.text: A patient shows sternal and intercostal retractions during breathing. This indicates:
options: Severe respiratory distress with airway obstruction
questions.explanation: Retractions (skin sucking in between ribs) indicate the patient is using accessory muscles due to severe respiratory distress.

questions.text: What is the purpose of an incentive spirometer?
options: To provide visual feedback for deep breathing exercises
questions.explanation: An incentive spirometer provides visual feedback to encourage deep breathing and prevent atelectasis.

questions.text: CPAP and BiPAP are examples of:
questions.explanation: CPAP and BiPAP are non-invasive methods that use positive pressure to physically force the lungs open.

questions.text: What is the primary goal when treating any respiratory emergency?
options: Identify and treat the underlying cause
questions.explanation: The core principle is to immediately identify and treat the underlying cause, whether infection, obstruction, or other pathology.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient is experiencing an acute asthma exacerbation. Expected assessment findings include: Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Asthma exacerbation features wheezing, tachypnea, chest tightness, and accessory muscle use. Tachycardia (not bradycardia) occurs due to distress and albuterol use.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which interventions are essential in the acute management of ischemic stroke? Select all that apply.
options: Obtaining a stat CT scan of the head.
Administering tPA within a 3 to 4.5 hour window if indicated.
Targeting a systolic blood pressure below 110 mm Hg.
Performing a rapid neurological assessment (e.g., NIHSS).
Keeping the patient NPO until a swallow screen is performed.
questions.explanation: CT scan, tPA protocol, NIHSS, and NPO status are critical. Extreme lowering of BP decreases cerebral perfusion; permissive hypertension is often allowed initially.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Classic signs of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) include: Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: DKA presents with hyperglycemia (not hypo-), Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, ketones, and severe dehydration.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following medications are considered high-alert medications and require at least independent double-checks before administration? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: High-alert medications carry a high risk of causing significant patient harm if used in error. Examples include IV insulin, IV anticoagulants (heparin), and IV concentrated electrolytes (potassium chloride).

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse is teaching a client with asthma about using a metered-dose inhaler (MDI). Which steps are correct? Select all that apply.
options: Shake the inhaler vigorously before use
Inhale rapidly and deeply while pressing the canister
Hold breath for 10 seconds after inhaling
Wait 1 minute between puffs if multiple puffs are ordered
Rinse the mouth with water after using a corticosteroid inhaler
questions.explanation: Correct MDI use includes shaking the inhaler, inhaling *slowly* and deeply, holding the breath for 10 seconds, waiting 1 minute between puffs, and rinsing the mouth after corticosteroid use to prevent thrush.

title: Endocrine & Critical Care - Part B
description: Diabetes, DKA, HHNS, Critical Assessment, Interventions

questions.text: What is the fundamental problem in diabetes mellitus?
options: Problem with insulin - the key that lets glucose into cells
questions.explanation: Diabetes is fundamentally a problem with insulin, which acts as the key allowing glucose to enter cells for fuel.

questions.text: Type 1 diabetes is characterized by:
options: Absolute insulin deficiency due to destroyed pancreatic beta cells
questions.explanation: Type 1 is an absolute insulin deficiency from destroyed beta cells, requiring lifelong exogenous insulin.

questions.text: Type 2 diabetes initially involves:
options: Insulin resistance first, then inadequate production later
questions.explanation: Type 2 starts with insulin resistance, then progresses to inadequate insulin production. Usually managed with diet, exercise, and oral medications.

questions.text: What are the 'three polys' of diabetes?
questions.explanation: The classic signs are polyuria (frequent urination), polydipsia (excessive thirst), and polyphagia (excessive hunger).

questions.text: DKA (diabetic ketoacidosis) is typically a complication of which type of diabetes?
questions.explanation: DKA typically occurs in Type 1 diabetes due to absolute lack of insulin, causing the body to break down fat for fuel.

questions.text: In DKA, why does the body break down fat?
options: Because cells can't get glucose without insulin, so the body thinks it's starving
questions.explanation: Without insulin, cells can't access glucose. The body perceives starvation and breaks down fat for alternative fuel.

questions.text: What are ketones and why are they dangerous?
options: Highly acidic byproducts of fat breakdown that cause metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: Ketones are acidic byproducts of fat metabolism. When they accumulate, they overwhelm buffers and cause severe metabolic acidosis.

questions.text: What is Kussmaul breathing and what does it indicate?
options: Deep, labored, rapid breaths attempting to blow off CO2 and raise pH
questions.explanation: Kussmaul respirations are deep, rapid breaths - the body's attempt to compensate for acidosis by exhaling CO2 (an acid).

questions.text: What causes the fruity breath odor in DKA?
questions.explanation: The fruity smell is acetone, a volatile ketone being exhaled through the lungs.

questions.text: What lab values confirm DKA?
questions.explanation: DKA is confirmed by glucose >250 mg/dL, pH <7.3, and presence of ketones in blood or urine.

questions.text: What is the FIRST priority in treating DKA?
options: Aggressive fluid resuscitation to correct dehydration
questions.explanation: Fluids first! Must correct severe dehydration and poor perfusion before starting insulin therapy.

questions.text: Why is it dangerous to give insulin before addressing potassium in DKA?
options: Insulin drives potassium into cells, potentially causing fatal hypokalemia
questions.explanation: Insulin shifts potassium into cells. If serum potassium is already low, insulin can cause dangerous or fatal hypokalemia.

questions.text: What are the three priorities in DKA treatment, in order?
questions.explanation: The order is critical: 1) Fluids to correct dehydration, 2) IV insulin to stop ketone production, 3) Potassium replacement.

questions.text: HHNS (hyperosmolar hyperglycemic non-ketotic syndrome) typically occurs in which type of diabetes?
questions.explanation: HHNS typically occurs in Type 2 diabetes, where there's enough insulin to prevent ketone formation but not enough to control glucose.

questions.text: What is the key difference between DKA and HHNS?
questions.explanation: HHNS shares high glucose and dehydration with DKA, but lacks ketones and acidosis because some insulin is present.

questions.text: In HHNS, glucose levels can reach:
questions.explanation: HHNS can have astronomical glucose levels, sometimes exceeding 600 mg/dL, causing profound dehydration.

questions.text: What is the primary intervention focus in HHNS?
options: Massive fluid resuscitation first, then controlled insulin
questions.explanation: HHNS is primarily a dehydration crisis. Treatment focuses on aggressive fluid replacement, then controlled insulin administration.

questions.text: Why is respiratory failure described as a 'common lethal endpoint' for many crises?
options: Multiple system failures (seizures, DKA, COPD) all can lead to respiratory collapse
questions.explanation: Whether from status epilepticus exhaustion, DKA compensation, or COPD structural failure, many crises end in respiratory failure.

questions.text: A patient in DKA shows signs of volume depletion. What causes this?
options: Osmotic pull of high glucose in urine causing massive fluid loss
questions.explanation: High glucose in urine creates osmotic pull, dragging water out and causing severe dehydration, hypotension, and tachycardia.

questions.text: What connects the respiratory and endocrine systems in DKA?
options: Kussmaul breathing is the respiratory system compensating for metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: The respiratory system attempts to compensate for DKA's metabolic acidosis through Kussmaul breathing to blow off CO2.

questions.text: What assessment finding would indicate successful DKA treatment?
options: Normalizing pH, decreasing glucose, improved mental status
questions.explanation: Successful treatment shows normalizing pH, decreasing glucose levels, clearing ketones, and improved mental status.

questions.text: Why must glucose be monitored closely during DKA treatment?
options: To prevent hypoglycemia as insulin works and to adjust treatment
questions.explanation: As insulin lowers glucose, close monitoring prevents hypoglycemia and guides treatment adjustments.

questions.text: What role does the neurological system play in recognizing metabolic crises?
options: Altered mental status signals severe metabolic derangement
questions.explanation: Changes in mental status (confusion, lethargy, coma) are critical signs of severe metabolic disturbance in DKA/HHNS.

questions.text: A patient with diabetes presents with confusion. What should be assessed first?
questions.explanation: In diabetic patients with altered mental status, immediately check blood glucose to rule out hypo- or hyperglycemia.

questions.text: What makes the interconnection of respiratory, neurological, and endocrine systems clinically significant?
options: Failure in one system rapidly affects the others, creating cascading emergencies
questions.explanation: These systems are profoundly interconnected - failure in one (e.g., DKA) immediately impacts others (respiratory compensation, altered mental status).

questions.text: What is the clinical significance of recognizing Kussmaul breathing?
options: It instantly signals severe metabolic acidosis requiring immediate intervention
It means the patient is anxious
questions.explanation: Kussmaul breathing is a critical red flag instantly indicating severe acidosis (likely DKA) requiring emergency treatment.

questions.text: Why is 'time is brain' emphasized in stroke care?
options: Every minute without treatment causes more irreversible brain damage
questions.explanation: Brain tissue dies rapidly without blood flow. Every minute of delay causes more permanent damage, making rapid intervention critical.

questions.text: What principle guides all critical care interventions discussed?
options: Identify and treat the underlying cause immediately
questions.explanation: The core principle across all emergencies is to immediately identify and treat the underlying cause, not just mask symptoms.

questions.text: How do assessment flags like pulsus paradoxus or Kussmaul breathing help clinicians?
options: They instantly reveal underlying pathology and dictate immediate life-saving interventions
questions.explanation: These clinical flags instantly clue you into the underlying crisis and tell you exactly what intervention is needed to save the patient.

questions.text: What makes understanding system interconnections essential for nursing practice?
options: It transforms facts into a clinical roadmap for recognizing and treating cascading emergencies
questions.explanation: Understanding how systems interact transforms isolated facts into an actionable clinical roadmap for managing complex, multi-system emergencies.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient with COPD is admitted with increased dyspnea. The nurse should: Select all that apply.
options: Advise the patient to use pursed-lip breathing.
Position the patient in a High-Fowler's position.
questions.explanation: Positioning, bronchodilators, and pursed-lip breathing are correct. Target SpO2 for COPD is usually 88-92% to prevent blunting the hypoxic drive. Shallow breathing is ineffective.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Signs of increased intracranial pressure (ICP) in an adult include: Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: LOC changes, Cushing's triad, pupil changes, and projectile vomiting indicate increased ICP. Bulging fontanelles only occur in infants whose skull sutures haven't fused.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A client with chronic kidney disease (CKD) is receiving hemodialysis. Which dietary instructions should the nurse provide? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Clients on hemodialysis should restrict fluids, sodium, potassium, and phosphorus. Protein intake is usually individualized but often needs to be adequate (not excessively high or low) to replace losses during dialysis, but limiting phosphorus and sodium is key.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse is teaching a client with asthma about using a metered-dose inhaler (MDI). Which steps are correct? Select all that apply.
options: Shake the inhaler vigorously before use
Inhale rapidly and deeply while pressing the canister
Hold breath for 10 seconds after inhaling
Wait 1 minute between puffs if multiple puffs are ordered
Rinse the mouth with water after using a corticosteroid inhaler
questions.explanation: Correct MDI use includes shaking the inhaler, inhaling *slowly* and deeply, holding the breath for 10 seconds, waiting 1 minute between puffs, and rinsing the mouth after corticosteroid use to prevent thrush.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A client is prescribed a broad-spectrum antibiotic. The nurse should monitor for which of the following superinfections? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Broad-spectrum antibiotics can alter normal flora, leading to superinfections like oral candidiasis, C. diff, and vaginal yeast infections. MRSA and S. pneumoniae are not typically classified as superinfections in this context.

title: Heme & GI Pathologies - Part A
description: Anemia, Sickle Cell, Coagulation Disorders (DIC, HIT, Hemophilia)

questions.text: What is the primary cause of pain in a Sickle Cell Vaso-Occlusive Crisis?
options: Tissue Ischemia caused by sickled cells blocking blood flow
questions.explanation: Sickled cells plug small vessels, starving tissues of oxygen.

questions.text: What is a 'Vaso-Occlusive Crisis'?
options: Severe pain episode due to sickled cells blocking microcirculation
questions.explanation: The hallmark emergency of Sickle Cell Disease.

questions.text: Which condition is characterized by a deficiency in Factor VIII?
questions.explanation: Classic Hemophilia (A) = Factor Eight (Eight sounds like A).

questions.text: Hemophilia B is a deficiency in:
questions.explanation: Christmas Disease = Factor IX.

questions.text: What is the classic sign of deep bleeding in Hemophilia?
questions.explanation: Repeated joint bleeds cause chronic deformity and pain.

questions.text: Von Willebrand Disease is a defect in:
questions.explanation: VWF binds platelets to the vessel wall injury.

questions.text: What is the paradox of HIT (Heparin Induced Thrombocytopenia)?
questions.explanation: Antibodies activate platelets, causing them to clump (clot) and get used up (low count).

questions.text: The immediate intervention for HIT is:
options: Stop Heparin immediately and switch to a direct thrombin inhibitor (e.g., Argatroban)
questions.explanation: Stop the trigger. Never give platelets (feeds the fire).

questions.text: DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation) is typically triggered by:
options: Severe Sepsis, Trauma, or Obstetric complications
questions.explanation: It's a secondary complication of a major shock to the system.

questions.text: In DIC, patients are at risk for:
options: Both massive Clotting and massive Hemorrhage simultaneously
questions.explanation: Clotting factors are used up in micro-clots, leading to bleeding everywhere else.

questions.text: Common signs of anemia include:
questions.explanation: Lack of RBCs means lack of Oxygen -> Heart beats faster to compensate.

questions.text: Neutropenia is defined as an Absolute Neutrophil Count (ANC) below:
questions.explanation: < 500 is critical neutropenia. Infection risk is extreme.

questions.text: For a neutropenic patient, a fever is:
options: A medical emergency requiring immediate antibiotics
questions.explanation: They have no immune system to fight. Sepsis can kill in hours.

questions.text: What type of blood product is used for clotting factor deficiencies (like bleeding in DIC)?
questions.explanation: FFP contains all clotting factors. Cryo is concentrated Fibrinogen/Factor VIII.

questions.text: How long can a unit of PRBCs hang for transfusion before it must be taken down?
questions.explanation: After 4 hours, bacterial growth risk increases significantly.

questions.text: The most common congenital immunodeficiency is:
questions.explanation: Often asymptomatic but carries risk of anaphylaxis with infusions.

questions.text: Patients with IgA deficiency are at high risk for what during blood transfusion?
questions.explanation: Their body attacks the IgA in the donor blood.

questions.text: What is TRALI?
questions.explanation: Sudden pulmonary edema after transfusion (non-cardiac origin).

questions.text: What is the first nursing action if a transfusion reaction is suspected?
questions.explanation: Stop the input of the allergen/toxin first.

questions.text: Iron Overload (Hemosiderosis) is a complication of:
questions.explanation: The body has no way to excrete excess iron from transfused cells.

questions.text: What medication is used for Iron Overload?
questions.explanation: Chelators bind the iron so it can be excreted.

questions.text: ITP (Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura) is caused by:
questions.explanation: The spleen destroys antibody-coated platelets.

questions.text: Why are platelet transfusions generally AVOIDED in ITP unless life-threatening?
options: The antibodies will just destroy the new platelets immediately
questions.explanation: It's like pouring water into a bucket with a hole. You treat the immune system instead (Steroids/IVIG).

questions.text: What gauge IV is preferred for blood transfusion?
questions.explanation: Larger bore prevents hemolysis (destruction) of red cells during infusion.

questions.text: Which saline is the ONLY compatible fluid with blood products?
questions.explanation: Dextrose causes hemolysis. Calcium in LR causes clotting.

questions.text: What is polycythemia?
options: Too many Red Blood Cells (Thick blood)
questions.explanation: Viscous blood risks clotting. Treat with phlebotomy.

questions.text: Pancytopenia means:
options: Low RBCs, Low WBCs, and Low Platelets (All cell lines low)
questions.explanation: Bone marrow failure (Aplastic Anemia).

questions.text: The Schilling Test is used to diagnose:
questions.explanation: Tests B12 absorption.

questions.text: What is the priority instruction for a patient taking liquid iron supplements?
options: Drink through a straw to prevent staining teeth
questions.explanation: Iron stains teeth black.

questions.text: Taking Iron with Vitamin C (Orange Juice) helps with:
questions.explanation: Acidic environment increases Iron absorption.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] During a blood transfusion, a patient experiences acute back pain, chills, and fever. The nurse suspects a hemolytic reaction. The immediate nursing actions should be: Select all that apply.
options: Flush the IV line with the remaining blood to ensure no medication is wasted.
Maintain patent IV access with normal saline using new tubing.
Notify the blood bank and healthcare provider.
Collect a urine sample to check for hemoglobinuria.
questions.explanation: Stop the blood immediately, run NS with new tubing, notify the provider/blood bank, and collect labs/urine. Do not flush the remaining blood into the patient.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient has severe liver cirrhosis. The nurse should monitor for which complications? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Cirrhosis causes portal hypertension (ascites, varices), toxin buildup (encephalopathy), and liver dysfunction leading to low platelets and coagulopathy.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which dietary modifications are recommended for a patient with inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's or Ulcerative Colitis) during an acute exacerbation? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: During flares, the bowel needs rest: low-residue, high-protein, and high-calorie diets are best. Fiber, raw fruits, and spices irritate the bowel.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following are appropriate nursing interventions for a client experiencing a tonic-clonic seizure? Select all that apply.
options: Insert a padded tongue blade into the clients mouth
Turn the client to the side
Protect the clients head from injury
questions.explanation: During a seizure, the nurse should turn the client to the side, loosen clothing, and protect the head. Inserting objects into the mouth and restraining the client can cause injury and are contraindicated.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] The nurse is caring for a client with a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) receiving a heparin infusion. Which actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
options: Massage the affected leg to promote circulation
Apply a warm, moist compress to the affected area
questions.explanation: For a DVT, the nurse should monitor aPTT, elevate the leg, assess for bleeding, and apply a warm compress. Massaging the leg is contraindicated as it can dislodge the clot.

title: GI & Hepatic Pathologies - Part B
description: IBD (UC vs Crohn's), Liver Cirrhosis, Biliary Atresia, Peritonitis

questions.text: The key anatomical difference between Ulcerative Colitis (UC) and Crohn's Disease is:
options: UC is widespread; Crohn's is local
UC is continuous inflammation of the colon/rectum (mucosal layer); Crohn's is 'skip lesions' anywhere in GI tract (transmural)
UC affects the mouth; Crohn's affects the stomach
questions.explanation: UC = Colon only, superficial. Crohn's = Mouth to Anus, deep fissures.

questions.text: A life-threatening complication of Ulcerative Colitis causing rapid colon dilation is:
questions.explanation: The colon dilates, paralyzes, and can rupture. Emergency.

questions.text: Because Crohn's Disease is transmural (goes through the wall), patients are at high risk for:
questions.explanation: Deep inflammation creates tunnels between loops of bowel or to the skin/bladder.

questions.text: The classic triad of signs for Biliary Atresia in infants includes:
options: Jaundice (>2 weeks), Dark Urine, and Light/Clay-colored Stools
questions.explanation: Bile is blocked from entering the gut (pale stool) and backs up into blood (jaundice/dark urine).

questions.text: Biliary Atresia requires which surgical intervention?
questions.explanation: The Kasai procedure connects the bowel directly to the liver to drain bile.

questions.text: Hepatic Encephalopathy in cirrhosis is caused by high levels of:
questions.explanation: The liver can't convert ammonia (toxic) to urea. Ammonia crosses the Blood-Brain Barrier.

questions.text: The primary medication used to treat Hepatic Encephalopathy by trapping ammonia is:
questions.explanation: Lactulose causes diarrhea, excreting ammonia in the stool.

questions.text: What is the hallmark sign of Peritonitis (Acute Abdomen)?
options: Rigid, board-like abdomen and rebound tenderness
questions.explanation: Severe inflammation of the lining causes muscles to spasm (guarding/rigidity).

questions.text: Esophageal Varices are caused by:
options: Portal Hypertension (High pressure in liver veins backing up)
questions.explanation: Cirrhotic liver increases pressure, blowing up collateral veins in the throat.

questions.text: If Esophageal Varices rupture, the priority is:
options: Airway protection and Hemodynamic stability (Shock management)
questions.explanation: Massive hemorrhage + Airway threat. Life-threatening.

questions.text: Which diet is recommended during an acute exacerbation of IBD (Crohn's/UC)?
options: Low Residue/Low Fiber, High Protein, High Calorie
questions.explanation: Rest the bowel. Avoid fiber which increases bulk and work.

questions.text: Ascites in liver failure is primarily caused by:
options: Portal Hypertension and Low Albumin (decreased oncotic pressure)
questions.explanation: Fluid pushes out due to pressure and isn't pulled back in due to lack of protein.

questions.text: A Sengstaken-Blakemore tube is used for:
questions.explanation: Emergency temporary measure to stop bleeding varices via balloon pressure.

questions.text: Asterixis ('Liver Flap') is a sign of:
questions.explanation: Hand-flapping tremor when wrist is extended.

questions.text: Which position is best for a patient after a Liver Biopsy?
options: Right Side (to compress the puncture site)
questions.explanation: Liver is on the right. Lying on it applies pressure to stop bleeding.

questions.text: Spider Angiomas and Palmar Erythema are skin signs of:
questions.explanation: Caused by high estrogen levels that the liver fails to metabolize.

questions.text: Murphy's Sign (pain on inspiration with RUQ palpation) suggests:
questions.explanation: The diaphragm pushes the inflamed gallbladder down into your fingers.

questions.text: McBurney's Point is associated with:
questions.explanation: Classic location for Appendix pain.

questions.text: Cullen's Sign (blue discoloration around umbilicus) indicates:
questions.explanation: Hemoperitoneum.

questions.text: Steatorrhea (fatty, foul-smelling stool) is a sign of:
questions.explanation: Fat is not being digested/absorbed.

questions.text: Pancreatitis pain is typically described as:
options: Severe, boring pain radiating to the back
questions.explanation: The pancreas is retroperitoneal, so pain goes straight through to the back.

questions.text: In Pyloric Stenosis (infants), the classic symptom is:
questions.explanation: The muscular ring closes off the stomach exit, ejecting food forcefully.

questions.text: Olive-shaped mass in the RUQ is pathognomonic for:
questions.explanation: The hypertrophied pyloric muscle feels like an olive.

questions.text: Intussusception (telescoping bowel) usually presents with:
options: Currant-Jelly Stool (blood + mucus) and sausage-shaped mass
questions.explanation: Classic pediatric emergency signs.

questions.text: Hirschsprung's Disease is characterized by:
options: Megacolon due to lack of nerve cells (aganglionic section) causing constipation/ribbon stools
questions.explanation: No nerves = No peristalsis = Blockage.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient with sickle cell crisis requires prioritizing which interventions? Select all that apply.
options: Applying cold compresses to painful joints.
questions.explanation: Hydration, oxygen, and pain relief are the priorities (HOP to it: Hydration, Oxygenation, Pain relief). Cold compresses cause further vasoconstriction.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Symptoms of a severe upper gastrointestinal (GI) bleed include: Select all that apply.
options: Hematochezia (bright red blood in stool, typical of lower GI).
questions.explanation: Upper GI bleeds cause hematemesis and melena, leading to shock (hypotension, tachycardia). Hematochezia is primarily seen in lower GI bleeds or massive, rapid upper bleeds.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following are appropriate nursing interventions for a client experiencing a tonic-clonic seizure? Select all that apply.
options: Insert a padded tongue blade into the clients mouth
Turn the client to the side
Protect the clients head from injury
questions.explanation: During a seizure, the nurse should turn the client to the side, loosen clothing, and protect the head. Inserting objects into the mouth and restraining the client can cause injury and are contraindicated.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse is caring for a client with liver cirrhosis. Which findings indicate the development of hepatic encephalopathy? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Hepatic encephalopathy is characterized by neurological changes due to ammonia buildup, resulting in asterixis (flapping tremor), altered mental status, and fetor hepaticus (musty breath). Jaundice and ascites are signs of cirrhosis but not specific to encephalopathy.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following medications are considered high-alert medications and require at least independent double-checks before administration? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: High-alert medications carry a high risk of causing significant patient harm if used in error. Examples include IV insulin, IV anticoagulants (heparin), and IV concentrated electrolytes (potassium chloride).

title: Renal Pathophysiology - Part A
description: AKI vs CKD, Pyelonephritis, Dialysis (Hemo vs Peritoneal)

questions.text: The primary difference between Cystitis and Pyelonephritis is:
options: Cystitis is viral; Pyelonephritis is bacterial
Cystitis involves the bladder (lower tract); Pyelonephritis involves the kidney parenchyma (upper tract)
questions.explanation: Pyelonephritis is an organ-level infection that can lead to sepsis.

questions.text: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is often reversible if potential causes (Prerenal, Intrarenal, Postrenal) are addressed. What defines 'Prerenal' failure?
options: Decreased perfusion/blood flow to the kidney (e.g., dehydration, heart failure)
questions.explanation: The 'pump' (heart/volume) fails to get blood to the filter.

questions.text: 'Postrenal' failure is caused by:
options: Obstruction of urine outflow (e.g., BPH, kidney stones)
questions.explanation: Back-pressure from a blockage damages the kidney.

questions.text: Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN) is a common cause of Intrarenal failure. It is triggered by:
options: Ischemia or Nephrotoxins (contrast dye, certain antibiotics)
questions.explanation: The cells inside the kidney die due to poisons or lack of oxygen.

questions.text: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) becomes noticeable when:
questions.explanation: The kidneys are resilient; symptoms are late-stage.

questions.text: Why does renal failure cause Anemia?
options: Lack of Erythropoietin (EPO) production by the kidney
questions.explanation: The kidney signals the bone marrow to make RBCs. No kidney = No signal.

questions.text: Peritoneal Dialysis uses what as the filter membrane?
options: The lining of the abdominal cavity (Peritoneum)
questions.explanation: Dialysate sits in the abdomen, pulling waste across the membrane.

questions.text: The major risk with Peritoneal Dialysis is:
questions.explanation: Bacteria can enter via the catheter, causing a serious infection. Cloudy outflow = Bad.

questions.text: Hemodialysis requires vascular access, typically an AV Fistula. What is a key assessment for a fistula?
options: Feel the 'Thrill' (vibration) and hear the 'Bruit' (swishing sound)
questions.explanation: Thrill and Bruit confirm patency and high flow.

questions.text: What is the single most important rule for Urinary Catheter care to prevent infection?
options: Keep the collection bag below the level of the bladder (maintain dependent flow)
questions.explanation: Prevent backflow of urine. Gravity is your friend.

questions.text: Securing the catheter to the leg prevents 'Pistoning', which is:
options: In-and-out movement that introduces bacteria into the urethra
questions.explanation: Micro-trauma creates entry points for germs.

questions.text: Uremic Syndrome (in End-Stage Renal Disease) affects:
options: Every body system (Neuro, GI, Skin, Heme, Bone)
questions.explanation: Waste products circulate everywhere, 'poisoning' the whole body.

questions.text: High Ammonia levels in liver/renal failure cause what skin sign?
questions.explanation: (Note: Actually Uremic Frost is urea crystals, Ammonia causes encephalopathy. But Uremic Frost is the skin sign of severe ESRD).

questions.text: Patients on Hemodialysis are at high risk for:
questions.explanation: Rapid fluid removal can crash blood pressure.

questions.text: What does a 'Closed Drainage System' mean for a catheter?
options: The connection between tube and bag is never broken to prevent bacteria entry
questions.explanation: Breaking the seal invites bacteria.

questions.text: Rhabdomyolysis causes kidney damage via:
options: Myoglobin released from damaged muscle blocking renal tubules
questions.explanation: Muscle protein is toxic to kidneys.

questions.text: A key sign of Rhabdomyolysis is:
questions.explanation: That's the myoglobin.

questions.text: Oliguria is defined as:
questions.explanation: A sign of failing kidneys.

questions.text: Which electrolyte imbalance is life-threatening in Renal Failure?
questions.explanation: Kidneys can't excrete Potassium -> Arrhythmias -> Cardiac Arrest.

questions.text: Kayexalate is used to treat:
questions.explanation: It binds potassium in the gut and excretes it via stool.

questions.text: Nephrotic Syndrome is characterized by massive loss of what in urine?
questions.explanation: Leaky glomerulus lets protein escape -> Oedema.

questions.text: In Hemodialysis, 'Disequilibrium Syndrome' is caused by:
options: Rapid removal of solutes causing cerebral edema (brain swelling)
questions.explanation: Fluid shifts into brain cells. Watch for LOC changes/seizures.

questions.text: Why do renal patients get bone disease (Renal Osteodystrophy)?
options: Imbalanced Calcium/Phosphorus and lack of Vitamin D activation
questions.explanation: High Phos pulls Calcium from bones.

questions.text: A suprapubic catheter is inserted:
options: Surgically through the abdominal wall directly into the bladder
questions.explanation: Bypasses the urethra.

questions.text: The diet for a Renal Failure patient typically restricts:
questions.explanation: The kidneys can't filter the byproducts of these.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient with Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) exhibits oliguria. The nurse should monitor for which electrolyte imbalances? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Kidneys fail to excrete potassium and phosphorus, leading to high levels. High phosphorus binds calcium, causing hypocalcemia.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which signs indicate compartment syndrome in a patient with a fractured tibia in a cast? Select all that apply.
options: Pallor and pulselessness of the foot.
Pain with passive stretch of the toes.
questions.explanation: The 6 Ps of compartment syndrome: Pain (out of proportion/passive stretch), Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient is undergoing peritoneal dialysis. The nurse notes the effluent drainage is cloudy. This indicates: Select all that apply.
options: A normal finding during the first few exchanges.
The need to obtain a sample for culture and sensitivity.
The need to administer broad-spectrum antibiotics as ordered after sampling.
An indication to rapidly cool the dialysate.
questions.explanation: Cloudy effluent indicates peritonitis, requiring culture and antibiotics. Normal drainage should be clear/straw-colored. Dialysate is warmed, never cooled.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse assesses a client with hyperthyroidism. Which of the following manifestations are expected? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism speeds up metabolism, leading to weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, diarrhea, and exophthalmos (bulging eyes). Constipation and weight gain are seen in hypothyroidism.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following are appropriate nursing interventions for a client experiencing a tonic-clonic seizure? Select all that apply.
options: Insert a padded tongue blade into the clients mouth
Turn the client to the side
Protect the clients head from injury
questions.explanation: During a seizure, the nurse should turn the client to the side, loosen clothing, and protect the head. Inserting objects into the mouth and restraining the client can cause injury and are contraindicated.

title: Musculoskeletal & Trauma - Part B
description: Fractures, Compartment Syndrome, Fat Embolism, Amputation Care

questions.text: What is the RICE protocol for soft tissue injuries (sprains/strains)?
questions.explanation: Standard first aid to limit swelling and bleeding.

questions.text: The difference between an Open and Closed fracture is:
options: Open fractures break the skin (bone exposed); Closed do not
questions.explanation: Open fractures carry a massive risk of infection (Osteomyelitis).

questions.text: What is the priority assessment for a patient with a cast or splint?
options: Neurovascular Checks (Pulse, Color, Temp, Sensation, Movement)
questions.explanation: Ensures the cast isn't cutting off circulation (Compartment Syndrome).

questions.text: Compartment Syndrome differs from normal post-injury pain because:
options: It is disproportionately severe, persistent, and unrelieved by pain meds
questions.explanation: Pressure builds in the fascia, crushing nerves/vessels.

questions.text: The treatment for acute Compartment Syndrome is:
options: Fasciotomy (surgical slicing of the fascia to release pressure)
questions.explanation: Relieve the pressure before tissue dies.

questions.text: Fat Embolism Syndrome is a risk after:
options: Long Bone Fractures (Femur) or Pelvic Fractures
questions.explanation: Marrow fat escapes into the bloodstream and clogs lung capillaries.

questions.text: Classic signs of Fat Embolism include respiratory distress, confusion, and:
questions.explanation: The rash helps distinguish it from a regular PE.

questions.text: Why are pelvic fractures life-threatening?
options: High risk of massive internal hemorrhage due to vascularity
questions.explanation: You can bleed out into the pelvic cavity quickly.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a Pelvic Stabilizer/Binder?
options: To compress the pelvis, reducing volume and tamponading bleeding
questions.explanation: Mechanical pressure stops the internal bleed.

questions.text: When caring for a severed/amputated limb part for potential reattachment, you should:
options: Wrap in moist sterile gauze, place in bag, then place bag on ice water
questions.explanation: Never put directly on ice (causes frostbite/cellular death).

questions.text: What is the contraindication for using a tourniquet?
options: Sickle Cell Crisis (increases ischemia risk) or known DVT
questions.explanation: Balance saving life vs. saving limb. Standard tourniquet is for uncontrolled life-threatening bleed.

questions.text: A 'Greenstick' fracture is common in:
questions.explanation: Incomplete break, like a green twig.

questions.text: What is Buck's Traction used for?
options: Pre-operative hip fracture stabilization (reduces muscle spasm)
questions.explanation: Skin traction to pull the leg and stop spasms.

questions.text: In Skeletal Traction (pins in bone), the weights must:
questions.explanation: If weights touch the floor, traction is lost.

questions.text: Pin care for skeletal traction involves:
options: Cleaning with Chlorhexidine/Saline to prevent infection (Osteomyelitis)
questions.explanation: Direct path to the bone = High infection risk.

questions.text: What is 'Phantom Limb Pain'?
options: Real pain sensation perceived in the missing limb due to nerve pathways
questions.explanation: It is real pain. Treat it.

questions.text: To prevent hip contractures in an amputee, you should:
options: Elevate limb on pillows for days
Have them lie Prone (on stomach) several times a day
Sit in a chair all day
questions.explanation: Prone position stretches the hip flexors.

questions.text: A Jackson-Pratt (JP) drain is an example of:
questions.explanation: Squeezing the bulb creates vacuum suction.

questions.text: A Penrose drain is:
questions.explanation: Soft tube, drains onto dressing. High infection risk.

questions.text: The 6 P's of Neurovascular Assessment are:
options: Pain, Pulse, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia (Cold)
questions.explanation: Memorize these for any ortho injury.

questions.text: Poikilothermia means:
options: Inability to regulate temp (limb takes on temp of environment/cold)
questions.explanation: Sign of arterial occlusion.

questions.text: Hypovolemic Shock signs in a trauma patient:
questions.explanation: Body compensates for blood loss by pumping faster.

questions.text: Crush injuries release Myoglobin, causing:
questions.explanation: Clogs the kidneys.

questions.text: Early mobilization after fracture surgery prevents:
questions.explanation: Stasis causes clots.

questions.text: For a suspected spinal injury, the immediate action is:
questions.explanation: Prevent permanent paralysis.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which interventions are appropriate for a patient post-total hip arthroplasty to prevent dislocation? Select all that apply.
options: Use an abduction pillow between the legs.
Instruct the patient not to cross their legs.
Encourage the patient to bend past 90 degrees at the waist to tie shoes.
questions.explanation: Abduction, avoiding crossing legs, and using elevated seats prevent dislocation. Flexion past 90 degrees and internal rotation cause dislocation.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient develops a fat embolism syndrome after a femur fracture. Assessment findings include: Select all that apply.
options: Petechiae on the chest and neck.
Sudden onset of dyspnea and hypoxia.
questions.explanation: Classic triad of FES: Hypoxemia (dyspnea), Neurologic abnormalities (altered MS), and a Petechial rash. Tachycardia and hypotension usually occur.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse assesses a client with hyperthyroidism. Which of the following manifestations are expected? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism speeds up metabolism, leading to weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, diarrhea, and exophthalmos (bulging eyes). Constipation and weight gain are seen in hypothyroidism.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following tasks can a registered nurse (RN) appropriately delegate to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)? Select all that apply.
options: Assisting a stable client with ambulation
Emptying a Foley catheter drainage bag
Measuring vital signs on a stable client
questions.explanation: UAPs can perform routine, stable tasks like ambulation, emptying drains, and taking vital signs. Assessment and medication administration require licensed nursing personnel.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A client with chronic kidney disease (CKD) is receiving hemodialysis. Which dietary instructions should the nurse provide? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Clients on hemodialysis should restrict fluids, sodium, potassium, and phosphorus. Protein intake is usually individualized but often needs to be adequate (not excessively high or low) to replace losses during dialysis, but limiting phosphorus and sodium is key.

title: Metabolic & Electrolytes - Part A
description: Acid-Base Balance (ABGs), Electrolyte Imbalances (Na, K, Ca, Mg)

questions.text: The normal pH range for human blood is:
questions.explanation: A very narrow range; deviations affect enzyme function.

questions.text: In Acid-Base balance, the Lungs manage CO2 (acid) by breathing faster/slower. This response is:
questions.explanation: Respiratory compensation is fast. Kidney compensation is slow.

questions.text: Kussmaul Respirations (deep, rapid breathing) are a compensatory mechanism for:
questions.explanation: The body tries to blow off CO2 (acid) to fix the metabolic acidity.

questions.text: Respiratory Alkalosis (High pH, Low CO2) is commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: Blowing off too much CO2 makes you alkaline.

questions.text: Hyponatremia (Low Sodium) primarily affects which system due to cellular swelling?
questions.explanation: Water rushes into brain cells -> Cerebral Edema.

questions.text: Hypernatremia (High Sodium) often presents with:
options: Intense Thirst, Dry mucous membranes, Flushed skin
questions.explanation: You are 'pickled' / dried out.

questions.text: Hypokalemia (Low Potassium) is characterized by:
options: Flattened T waves / U waves and Muscle Weakness
questions.explanation: Heart muscle repolarization is delayed.

questions.text: The max rate for IV Potassium infusion via peripheral line is:
questions.explanation: Never push K+ IV push. It stops the heart. 10mEq/hr is safe max peripheral.

questions.text: Hyperkalemia (High Potassium) causes what dangerous ECG change?
questions.explanation: Precursor to V-Fib/Cardiac Arrest.

questions.text: The emergency treatment for severe Hyperkalemia to STABILIZE the heart membrane is:
questions.explanation: Calcium doesn't lower K+, it protects the heart muscle from stopping.

questions.text: To SHIFT potassium from blood back into cells, you administer:
questions.explanation: Insulin carries glucose and potassium into cells.

questions.text: Chvostek's Sign (face twitch) and Trousseau's Sign (carpal spasm) indicate:
questions.explanation: Neuromuscular irritability/tetany.

questions.text: Magnesium acts as the body's:
questions.explanation: Low Mag = Hyperactive reflexes. High Mag = Absent reflexes (too sedated).

questions.text: A patient with metabolic acidosis (e.g., kidney failure) will have:
questions.explanation: Metabolic = HCO3 matches pH direction (Both Low = Acidosis).

questions.text: Massive tissue damage (like Burns or Crush injuries) releases intracellular Potassium, causing:
questions.explanation: Cells burst open, spilling K+ into the blood.

questions.text: The 'Gold Standard' for assessing acid-base balance is:
questions.explanation: Measures pH, PaCO2, HCO3, PaO2 directly.

questions.text: Kayexalate works by:
options: Binding Potassium in the gut for excretion in stool
questions.explanation: The 'Removal' step (permanent lowering of K).

questions.text: Hypocalcemia can occur after surgery on which gland?
questions.explanation: Accidental damage to Parathyroids drops Calcium levels rapidly.

questions.text: Which condition causes Metabolic Alkalosis?
options: Prolonged Vomiting or NG Suction (Losing Acid)
questions.explanation: Losing stomach acid leaves you alkaline.

questions.text: Diarrhea typically causes:
questions.explanation: You lose 'Base' from the 'Basement' (Bowels) -> Acidic.

questions.text: Which organ manages Bicarbonate (HCO3)?
questions.explanation: The slow metabolic regulator.

questions.text: A patient hyperventilating from a panic attack should:
questions.explanation: Re-breathe CO2 to correct respiratory alkalosis.

questions.text: If a patient has High Magnesium (Hypermagnesemia), deep tendon reflexes will be:
questions.explanation: Magnesium Sedates.

questions.text: Which IV solution is Isotonic?
questions.explanation: It matches body fluid osmolality.

questions.text: Water intoxication (drinking too much water) leads to:
questions.explanation: Dilutes the sodium.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient with severe burns over 40% of their body is admitted. During the emergent (resuscitation) phase, the nurse expects which fluid and electrolyte shifts? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Capillary leak causes weeping of fluid (hyponatremia, hypotension), and cell destruction releases potassium (hyperkalemia). Urine output drops (oliguria), not polyuria.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which clinical manifestations are associated with Meniere's disease? Select all that apply.
options: Feeling of fullness in the affected ear.
questions.explanation: Meniere's triad includes vertigo, tinnitus, and hearing loss, along with aural fullness. Purulent discharge indicates infection.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient presents with acute angle-closure glaucoma. The nurse recognizes this as a medical emergency characterized by: Select all that apply.
options: Gradual loss of peripheral vision over years.
questions.explanation: Acute glaucoma causes sudden, severe pain, halos, redness, and N/V. Gradual peripheral visual loss occurs in chronic open-angle glaucoma.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse is caring for a client with liver cirrhosis. Which findings indicate the development of hepatic encephalopathy? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Hepatic encephalopathy is characterized by neurological changes due to ammonia buildup, resulting in asterixis (flapping tremor), altered mental status, and fetor hepaticus (musty breath). Jaundice and ascites are signs of cirrhosis but not specific to encephalopathy.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse assesses a client with hyperthyroidism. Which of the following manifestations are expected? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism speeds up metabolism, leading to weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, diarrhea, and exophthalmos (bulging eyes). Constipation and weight gain are seen in hypothyroidism.

title: Integumentary, Sensory & Neuro - Part B
description: Burns, Necrotizing Fasciitis, Meniere's, Temporal Arteritis

questions.text: The priority intervention for Chemical Extravasation (IV leak of vesicant) is:
options: Stop infusion immediately, secure line, elevate limb
questions.explanation: Limit the amount of toxic drug entering the tissue. Don't remove the catheter instantly—you might need it to inject an antidote.

questions.text: Necrotizing Fasciitis ('Flesh-eating disease') requires what immediate life-saving intervention?
questions.explanation: Antibiotics alone can't penetrate dead tissue. It must be cut out.

questions.text: What electrolyte imbalance is an immediate risk after a major burn?
questions.explanation: Massive cell destruction releases intracellular Potassium into the blood.

questions.text: A Wound Vac (Negative Pressure Wound Therapy) promotes healing by:
options: Removing excess fluid, reducing edema, and stimulating blood flow
questions.explanation: Negative pressure draws wound edges together and removes 'bad' fluid.

questions.text: Bell's Palsy affects which Cranial Nerve?
questions.explanation: Unilateral facial paralysis.

questions.text: Bell's Palsy is often linked to Herpes Simplex Virus. Treatment includes:
questions.explanation: Reduce inflammation and fight the virus.

questions.text: Temporal Arteritis (Giant Cell Arteritis) presents with a new headache, jaw pain, and:
questions.explanation: A medical emergency requiring immediate steroids to save vision.

questions.text: Meniere's Disease is characterized by a triad of symptoms:
options: Vertigo, Tinnitus, and Fluctuating Hearing Loss
questions.explanation: Fluid buildup in the inner ear.

questions.text: The diet for Meniere's Disease should be:
questions.explanation: Sodium retains water; you want to reduce fluid pressure in the ear.

questions.text: LeFort Fractures involve which part of the body?
questions.explanation: Major facial trauma.

questions.text: The priority intervention for a LeFort fracture (or any facial trauma) is:
questions.explanation: Swelling/bleeding can block the airway instantly.

questions.text: The 'Palm Slide Test' is used to check:
questions.explanation: If you can feel the bony prominence through the mattress, it's failing.

questions.text: Labyrinthitis differs from Meniere's because:
options: It is usually viral/acute and causes constant severe vertigo
questions.explanation: Inflammation of the inner ear structures.

questions.text: Cellulitis is:
options: Bacterial infection of the deeper skin layers (dermis/subcutaneous)
questions.explanation: Red, hot, swollen, painful.

questions.text: Otitis Media is an infection of the:
questions.explanation: Behind the eardrum.

questions.text: What precaution is taken for a patient with Temporal Arteritis?
questions.explanation: Don't wait for biopsy results. Save the eye.

questions.text: Jaw Claudication (pain when chewing) is a specific sign of:
questions.explanation: Ischemia of the masticator muscles.

questions.text: To prevent aspiration in a patient with facial trauma/wiring, keeping what at the bedside is critical?
questions.explanation: If they vomit with their jaw wired shut, you must cut the wires instantly.

questions.text: A 'Vesicant' medication:
options: Causes blistering and tissue necrosis if extravasated
questions.explanation: Handle with extreme care (e.g., Chemo).

questions.text: Which is a sign of Metabolic Acidosis?
questions.explanation: Compensatory hyperventilation.

questions.text: Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy is used for Necrotizing Fasciitis because:
options: It kills anaerobic bacteria and promotes healing
questions.explanation: Oxygen is toxic to anaerobes.

questions.text: Glaucoma is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Pressure damages the optic nerve.

questions.text: A patient with Meniere's is at high risk for:
questions.explanation: Safety is priority #1.

questions.text: The Tinnitus in Meniere's disease is typically described as:
questions.explanation: Classic symptom.

questions.text: Static pressure surfaces (foam/gel) differ from Dynamic because:
options: They do not move/inflate; they just redistribute weight
questions.explanation: Dynamic uses a pump.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient has a suspected diagnosis of temporal arteritis. The nurse anticipates which findings or interventions? Select all that apply.
options: Providing cold packs to the forehead for vasodilation.
questions.explanation: Temporal arteritis features temporal headaches, jaw pain, elevated markers (ESR), and requires immediate steroids to prevent blindness.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] When caring for a patient with a negative pressure wound therapy (wound vac) device, the nurse should: Select all that apply.
options: Ensure the foam dressing is collapsed and firm.
Monitor for continuous bubbling or hissing, indicating an air leak.
Turn off the machine for 4 hours daily to let the wound breathe.
Assess the drainage canister for amount and color of exudate.
Notify the provider if bright red bleeding suddenly appears.
questions.explanation: The dressing should shrink under vacuum, leaks must be fixed, and drainage monitored. The vac should NOT be turned off for extended periods.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A client is prescribed a broad-spectrum antibiotic. The nurse should monitor for which of the following superinfections? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Broad-spectrum antibiotics can alter normal flora, leading to superinfections like oral candidiasis, C. diff, and vaginal yeast infections. MRSA and S. pneumoniae are not typically classified as superinfections in this context.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A nurse is teaching a client with asthma about using a metered-dose inhaler (MDI). Which steps are correct? Select all that apply.
options: Shake the inhaler vigorously before use
Inhale rapidly and deeply while pressing the canister
Hold breath for 10 seconds after inhaling
Wait 1 minute between puffs if multiple puffs are ordered
Rinse the mouth with water after using a corticosteroid inhaler
questions.explanation: Correct MDI use includes shaking the inhaler, inhaling *slowly* and deeply, holding the breath for 10 seconds, waiting 1 minute between puffs, and rinsing the mouth after corticosteroid use to prevent thrush.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] Which of the following are classic signs of a myocardial infarction (MI)? Select all that apply.
options: Chest pain radiating to the left arm or jaw
Relief of pain with rest and nitroglycerin
questions.explanation: Classic MI signs include radiating chest pain, diaphoresis, dyspnea, and nausea. Pain relieved by rest and nitroglycerin is characteristic of stable angina, not an MI.

title: Sepsis & Multi-System Emergencies - Part A
description: SIRS, Septic Shock, DIC, Geriatric Atypical Presentation

questions.text: SIRS (Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome) requires at least 2 criteria. Which of the following is a criterion?
questions.explanation: Extremes of temp, Tachycardia (>90), Tachypnea, or abn WBC count.

questions.text: Sepsis is defined as:
questions.explanation: It's the systemic response to an infection.

questions.text: Septic Shock is distinguished from Severe Sepsis by:
options: Refractory Hypotension (Low BP persists despite aggressive fluid resuscitation)
questions.explanation: If fluids don't fix the BP, it's Shock. You need Vasopressors.

questions.text: The massive vasodilation in Distributive Shock (like Sepsis) causes:
questions.explanation: The vascular tone is lost, so pressure drops.

questions.text: DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation) is a complication of sepsis where:
options: Clotting factors are used up causing widespread clotting AND massive bleeding simultaneously
questions.explanation: A paradox: You bleed because you clotted too much elsewhere.

questions.text: The priority intervention window for starting antibiotics in sepsis is:
questions.explanation: Time is tissue. Mortality increases every hour delayed.

questions.text: If fluid resuscitation (30ml/kg) fails to restore MAP in Septic Shock, the next step is:
questions.explanation: Squeeze the pipes to maintain perfusion.

questions.text: The single most dangerous 'Red Flag' for acute illness in the elderly is:
questions.explanation: Acute Mental Status Change = Infection/Hypoxia until proven otherwise.

questions.text: Why might an elderly patient with severe sepsis NOT have a fever?
options: Blunted immune response / Inability to mount a fever
questions.explanation: Don't trust temperature in the elderly. Look for confusion.

questions.text: Delirium differs front Dementia because Delirium is:
questions.explanation: Delirium is a medical emergency.

questions.text: Neurogenic Shock is unique because it presents with Hypotension and:
questions.explanation: Loss of sympathetic tone means the heart rate doesn't speed up to compensate.

questions.text: Cardiogenic Shock is a failure of:
questions.explanation: The heart fails to squeeze. Do NOT overload with fluids.

questions.text: Hypovolemic Shock is caused by:
options: Loss of circulating volume (Hemorrhage or Dehydration)
questions.explanation: The tank is empty.

questions.text: MODS stands for:
questions.explanation: The endgame of uncorrected shock.

questions.text: A urinary output below what level indicates kidney failure/shock?
questions.explanation: Oliguria is the first sign of poor perfusion.

questions.text: Geriatric 'Frailty' is characterized by:
options: Unintentional weight loss, weakness, and slowed activity
questions.explanation: A state of high vulnerability to stressors.

questions.text: The most common manifestation of physical illness in the elderly is:
questions.explanation: A fall is rarely just an accident; investigate the 'why'.

questions.text: Treating Sepsis involves a 'Bundle' of care. Which comes FIRST?
questions.explanation: Measure Lactate, get blood cultures, start fluids/antibiotics.

questions.text: Refractory Hypotension in shock indicates:
options: Need for Vasopressors (Pipes are too dilapidated)
questions.explanation: Fluid alone isn't enough.

questions.text: In Anaphylactic Shock (a type of Distributive Shock), the priority is:
questions.explanation: Epinephrine reverses the vasodilation and bronchoconstriction.

questions.text: Severe Sepsis is SIRS + Infection + :
questions.explanation: Organ damage marks the transition to Severe.

questions.text: Class IV Hypovolemic Shock involves losing:
questions.explanation: Massive hemorrhage.

questions.text: Polypharmacy in the elderly increases the risk of:
questions.explanation: More drugs = More problems.

questions.text: A 'Shift to the Left' in WBC count indicates:
options: Acute Bacterial Infection (increase in immature neutrophils/bands)
questions.explanation: The marrow is pumping out baby WBCs to fight.

questions.text: Why is 'Time' tissue in Sepsis?
options: Delayed perfusion causes permanent organ death
questions.explanation: Every hour of hypotension increases mortality.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A patient is presenting with Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS). Which criteria confirm SIRS? Select all that apply.
options: Respiratory rate > 20 breaths/minute or PaCO2 < 32 mm Hg.
questions.explanation: SIRS criteria involve abnormal temp, HR, RR, and WBC. A positive culture with SIRS moves the diagnosis to Sepsis.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] In the management of septic shock, the '1-Hour Bundle' includes which actions? Select all that apply.
options: Obtain blood cultures before administering antibiotics.
Begin rapid administration of 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension.
questions.explanation: The bundle requires lactate, cultures prior to antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and fluid resuscitation. Transfusions are not standard initial prophylaxis.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] An older adult patient with no history of dementia is suddenly confused and agitated. The nurse suspects delirium over dementia because delirium: Select all that apply.
options: Is often accompanied by a fluctuating level of consciousness.
Can be caused by an underlying infection, such as a UTI.
Is characterized by a slow, insidious decline in memory over years.
questions.explanation: Delirium is acute, fluctuating, and often linked to physical illness (like a UTI). Dementia is slow, insidious, and progressive.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] A client is prescribed a broad-spectrum antibiotic. The nurse should monitor for which of the following superinfections? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Broad-spectrum antibiotics can alter normal flora, leading to superinfections like oral candidiasis, C. diff, and vaginal yeast infections. MRSA and S. pneumoniae are not typically classified as superinfections in this context.

questions.text: [SATA Challenge] The nurse is caring for a client with a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) receiving a heparin infusion. Which actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
options: Massage the affected leg to promote circulation
Apply a warm, moist compress to the affected area
questions.explanation: For a DVT, the nurse should monitor aPTT, elevate the leg, assess for bleeding, and apply a warm compress. Massaging the leg is contraindicated as it can dislodge the clot.

title: Maternal & OB Emergencies - Part B
description: Bleeding Disorders (Abruptio, Previa), PPH, Preeclampsia

questions.text: The primary symptom distinguishing Placenta PREVIA from Abruptio Placentae is:
questions.explanation: Previa is Painless. Abruption is Painful.

questions.text: What is the absolute contraindication in a patient with Placenta Previa?
questions.explanation: Poking the placenta can cause massive, uncontrollable hemorrhage.

questions.text: Abruptio Placentae (premature separation) presents with:
options: Painful vaginal bleeding and a rigid, board-like uterus
questions.explanation: The uterus contracts and bleeds internally. High fetal mortality.

questions.text: Severe Preeclampsia is characterized by BP > 160/110 and:
options: Proteinuria, Headache, Visual disturbances, Epigastric pain
